name: Fast & Slow Pointers
summary: Walk two cursors down the list in one pass: slow takes one hop, fast takes two. When fast runs out of list, slow has covered exactly half of it.
visualizationIntent: init: build the chain of nodes and their next-links; both cursors start together at the head. move: each pass slow hops ONCE (drawn as a single arc below the chain) while fast hops TWICE (drawn as a double arc above it) — the widening gap is the whole idea. loop: continue while fast still has a node and a next node. return: fast has fallen off the end, slow sits on the middle node, and the tail from slow onward lights up as the answer.

lineExplanations.3: Start an empty list of next-pointers, one per node.
lineExplanations.4: Index of the node whose link we're about to build.
lineExplanations.5: Build one link per node in the list.
lineExplanations.6: Node i points at node i+1 — the usual 'next' pointer.
lineExplanations.7: Move on to the next node.
lineExplanations.8: The LAST node points at nothing, so overwrite its link with -1 (null).
lineExplanations.9: The slow cursor starts at the head.
lineExplanations.10: The fast cursor also starts at the head — they begin together.
lineExplanations.11: Remembers where slow hopped from, so the visual can draw its single hop.
lineExplanations.12: Remembers where fast hopped from, so the visual can draw its DOUBLE hop.
lineExplanations.13: Counts how many rounds the two cursors have taken.
lineExplanations.15: Keep going while fast still has a node AND that node has a next — fast needs TWO nodes to move.
lineExplanations.16: Record where fast is standing before it hops.
lineExplanations.18: Fast's SECOND hop — this is why it covers ground twice as fast as slow.
lineExplanations.19: Record where slow is standing before it hops.
lineExplanations.20: Slow advances ONE node, closing back to exactly half of fast's distance.
lineExplanations.22: Fast has run out of list, so slow is exactly halfway. Return the list from slow onward.
lineExplanations.23: Hand back the middle node and everything after it.

syntaxExplanations.1: class Solution: is the class wrapper LeetCode expects.
syntaxExplanations.2: def middleNode(self, head): head is the list of node values; a real LeetCode call would pass a ListNode chain.
syntaxExplanations.3: [] creates an empty list that will hold each node's next-index.
syntaxExplanations.4: i = 0 starts at the first node.
syntaxExplanations.5: while i < len(head): runs once per node.
syntaxExplanations.6: append(i + 1) records that node i points at node i+1.
syntaxExplanations.7: i = i + 1 moves to the next node.
syntaxExplanations.8: len(head) - 1 is the last index; -1 is our stand-in for a null next-pointer.
syntaxExplanations.9: slow = 0 puts the slow cursor on the head node.
syntaxExplanations.10: fast = 0 puts the fast cursor on the head node too.
syntaxExplanations.11: slow_from = 0 declares the variable that tracks slow's previous position.
syntaxExplanations.12: fast_from = 0 declares the variable that tracks fast's previous position.
syntaxExplanations.14: result = [] declares the answer variable before the loop.
syntaxExplanations.15: 'and' short-circuits: if fast is already -1, Python never evaluates node_links[fast], which would be an error.
syntaxExplanations.16: fast_from = fast saves the position before moving.
syntaxExplanations.17: node_links[fast] is fast's next node — assigning it moves the cursor one step.
syntaxExplanations.18: Following the next-pointer a SECOND time in the same round is what doubles fast's speed.
syntaxExplanations.19: slow_from = slow saves the position before moving.
syntaxExplanations.20: The same next-pointer follow, applied to slow — but only once.
syntaxExplanations.22: head[slow:] is a slice — every value from index slow to the end.
syntaxExplanations.23: return result hands back the answer.
